content.text: Democracy is universally seen as a better form of government than dictatorship or any other alternative. It is supported because it promotes equality, enhances individual dignity, improves decision-making quality, provides methods to resolve conflicts, and allows room to correct mistakes.

content.title: A Form of Government
content.text: The first step in assessment is recognizing that democracy is just a form of government. It can only create conditions for achieving goals; it is not a magical solution to all socio-economic problems. Citizens must take advantage of these conditions to achieve progress.
The most basic outcome of democracy should be a government that is accountable to the citizens and responsive to their needs and expectations.

content.title: Deliberation vs Speed
items: Non-democratic rulers can be very fast as they do not bother with deliberation or public opinion.
Democracy is based on deliberation and negotiation, which may cause delays.
However, decisions taken through procedures are more likely to be acceptable and effective. The 'cost of time' in democracy is worth it.

content.title: Defining Transparency
content.text: A citizen has the right and the means to examine the process of decision-making. This is known as transparency. In a democracy, decisions are based on norms and procedures, and mechanisms exist to hold the government accountable.
A democratic government is 'people’s own government.' This is the primary reason for the overwhelming support for democracy across the world, especially in South Asia.

content.title: Legitimacy
content.text: While a democracy may be slow, less efficient, or not always clean, it is legitimate because it is elected by the people. People believe that democracy is suitable for their country.
Data from 1950 to 2000 shows that dictatorships have had a slightly higher rate of economic growth. However, this alone is not a reason to reject democracy.

content.title: Factors Affecting Economic Development
items: Global situation and cooperation from other countries.
Economic priorities adopted by the country.

content.title: The Verdict
content.text: The difference in rates of economic growth between less developed countries under dictatorship and democracy is negligible. Democracy is preferred because it has several other positive outcomes that dictatorship lacks.
Democracies are based on political equality (equal weight in electing representatives), but they often show growing economic inequalities.

content.title: Economic Disparities
items: A small number of ultra-rich enjoy a disproportionate share of wealth and income.
Those at the bottom have very little to depend on, and their incomes have been declining.
In actual life, democracies have not been very successful in reducing economic inequalities.

content.text: Democracies usually develop procedures to conduct their competition, which reduces the possibility of social tensions becoming explosive or violent. Ability to handle social differences is a major 'plus point' of democratic regimes.

content.title: Two Conditions for Social Harmony
items: Democracy is not simply rule by majority opinion. The majority must work with the minority so that the government represents the general view.
Rule by majority must not become rule by a 'majority community' (religion, race, or linguistic group). Democracy remains only as long as every citizen has a chance of being in the majority.

notes.section_title: Dignity and Freedom of the Citizens

content.text: Democracy stands superior to any other form of government in promoting the dignity and freedom of the individual. The passion for respect and freedom is the basis of democracy.

content.title: Women and Caste Equality
content.text: Long struggles by women and disadvantaged castes have created sensitivity today that equal treatment is a necessary ingredient of a democratic society. While discrimination still exists, it now lacks legal and moral foundations in a democracy.

What is most distinctive about democracy is that its examination never gets over. As it passes one test, it produces another. The fact that people are complaining is a testimony to the success of democracy.

content.title: From Subject to Citizen
content.text: Public expression of dissatisfaction shows that people have developed awareness. It transforms people from the status of a 'subject' into that of a 'citizen' who believes their vote makes a difference.

glossary.definition: The obligation of the government and its officials to answer for their decisions and actions to the citizens.
A government that is attentive to the needs and demands of the people and acts according to their expectations.
A government that is 'people's own government', having been elected through a democratic process and recognized by law.
The right and means for citizens to examine the process of decision-making and access information about government functioning.
The process of careful discussion and consideration of alternatives before taking a final decision.
In the context of democratic outcomes, the danger where rule by majority becomes rule by a single majority community (religion, race, etc.) rather than a fluid majority of opinions.
The state or quality of being worthy of honor or respect; in democracy, every individual is considered equal and deserving of respect.
Individuals who hold the ultimate power in a democracy and are the masters of their own political fate through their vote.

summary_points: Democracy is a better form of government as it promotes equality, enhances individual dignity, and improves decision-making quality.
The primary expectation from democracy is an accountable, responsive, and legitimate government that follows formal procedures.
Transparency, through mechanisms like the Right to Information, allows citizens to hold the government accountable for its decisions.
While dictatorships show slightly higher economic growth, democracy is preferred for its ability to accommodate social diversity and provide rights.
Democracies often struggle to reduce economic inequalities, with wealth remaining concentrated in the hands of a few ultra-rich individuals.
Successful democracy requires the majority to work with the minority and ensuring that 'majority rule' does not become communalism.
Democracy's greatest strength is its ability to recognize the dignity and freedom of every individual, transforming 'subjects' into 'citizens'.
Public dissatisfaction and complaints are signs of a successful democracy, as they reflect a critical and aware citizenry.
